metadata.summary: Create research-backed buyer personas from 10+ interviews with transcript citations, avoiding demographic theatre and hallucinated traits.

content.title: Persona Building
content.summary: A 5-step process for creating semi-fictional buyer archetypes grounded in real interview data: gather data (minimum 10 interviews), cluster patterns, define 1-3 segments (primary / secondary / negative), build the persona with cited traits, then validate by showing the doc to actual customers. Outputs user-personas.md in .aidocs/product_docs/.

why.p: Personas built from imagination produce "demographic theatre" — stock-photo characters that never change a real product or marketing decision. Research-backed personas with transcript citations force the team to confront actual user language, purchasing triggers, and frustrations, making positioning and onboarding decisions testable rather than aesthetic.

list.item: Raw transcripts from 8+ customer interviews exist and need clustering into buyer archetypes before writing positioning or landing copy.
Landing page has poor conversion and the team disagrees on "who this is for".
Sales closes one segment 3x faster than others — codify that segment as primary persona.
Pivoting into an adjacent market and needing a new primary persona before rewriting home page and pricing tiers.
A multi-agent pipeline needs a shared persona artifact as input (faion-research-agent → faion-marketing-manager → faion-content-marketer).
Pre-PMF / pre-interview: zero customer conversations — run user-interviews and pain-point-research first.
B2B enterprise with named-account selling and fewer than 20 logos — use account profiles and buying-committee maps.
Highly heterogeneous marketplaces where the segmentation axis is transactional behavior — use audience-segmentation instead.
Single-feature internal tools where the user is unambiguous — a 1-line role description beats a 2-page persona doc.

section.name: data-sources

rule.statement: Conduct a minimum of 10 customer interviews before creating any persona. Do not build personas from imagination or market assumptions.
rule.rationale: Personas without interview data produce "demographic theatre" — plausible-looking characters that never change a real decision. Ten interviews is the minimum for identifying patterns that recur across multiple people.

section.p: Data sources ranked by signal strength: interviews (goals, frustrations, workflow), surveys (demographics, tool usage), analytics (behavior, engagement), support tickets (problems, exact language used), social media (interests, how they talk), sales calls (objections, motivations).

section.name: pattern-clustering
section.p: Cluster interview findings on five dimensions: role/title, goals, frustrations, behaviors, buying triggers. Record both frequency (how many people said this) and intensity (how strongly they expressed it).

rule.statement: Every trait in the final persona must have a transcript citation in the format [transcript-id:line]. Traits with fewer than 3 supporting citations across transcripts are marked [WEAK] and excluded from the primary persona.
rule.rationale: Uncited traits are hypotheses, not findings. Marking weak traits prevents them from driving product decisions while keeping them visible for future validation.

section.name: segment-types

rule.statement: Start with ONE primary persona. Add secondary personas only after the product has traction. Define a negative persona (who NOT to target) from the start.
rule.rationale: Multiple personas at seed stage split focus and produce muddled positioning. The negative persona is more decision-shaping than the primary — "who do we say no to?" is the harder and more valuable question.

section.p: Segment types: Primary (main buyer, most revenue — focus all design here), Secondary (important but smaller — support their needs), Negative (explicitly not for them — do not market to, politely decline in sales).

section.name: persona-elements
section.p: Essential elements (required): name (memorable, role-based), role (job title/situation), demographics (only if 5+ transcripts agree), goals (what success looks like), frustrations (what blocks them), behaviors (how they work and buy), quote (verbatim from interview).
Advanced elements (add only when handing off to marketing or design): day in the life, tools used, information sources, objections, buying trigger.

template.purpose: Full persona template with all fields

section.name: validation

rule.statement: Show the persona to 3 real customers and ask "does this sound like you?" before committing the document to the repository. If 2 of 3 say "no", rebuild.
rule.rationale: Personas validated only internally often reflect team assumptions rather than customer reality. External validation catches the most common failure mode: the team's mental model of the customer diverges from the actual customer.

section.p: Update frequency: major update quarterly, minor tweaks as you learn, full rebuild after pivot or entry into a new market.

example.note: Primary persona for a SaaS tool targeting freelance writers. All traits sourced from interviews.
example.code:
## Newsletter Nate
**Who:** 34-year-old freelance writer, runs paid newsletter with 2,000 subscribers, makes $4K/month from writing.
**Goal:** Grow subscriber base to 10,000 and reduce time on operations.
**Frustration:** Email platform is expensive at scale; no easy way to sell digital products; analytics are confusing.
**Quote:** "I'm a writer, not a tech person. I just want it to work." [T03:L87]
**Buys when:** Current tool fails or becomes too expensive on price jump.
**Based on:** 12 interviews (all freelance writers with paid newsletters)

example.note: Primary persona for a project management tool targeting 5-person design agencies.
example.code:
## Agency Amy
**Who:** 38-year-old agency owner, 5-person design agency, $500K annual revenue.
**Goals:** Stop projects going over budget; improve client communication; scale without overhead.
**Frustrations:** Team uses 6 different tools; clients don't understand project status; time tracking is inconsistent.
**Quote:** "I need my team aligned without micromanaging." [T07:L42]
**Buys when:** A project goes over budget for the third time in a quarter.
**Based on:** 15 interviews (agency owners, 3-15 employees)

antipattern.description: Creating personas with no interview data, using demographics or market reports as the source.
antipattern.reason: Fabricated personas produce wrong product decisions. A persona without transcript citations is a hypothesis dressed as a finding.

antipattern.description: Creating 4+ personas at seed stage and trying to design for all of them simultaneously.
antipattern.reason: Multiple personas split focus and produce muddled messaging. One primary persona until the product has traction and ARR to justify more.

antipattern.description: Including stock-photo demographics (age, city, salary) that no one on the team ever references in a product decision.
antipattern.reason: Demographic fields that never change a decision are dead weight. Include a demographic field only if it drives a specific product or messaging choice.

antipattern.description: Publishing a persona document and never updating it.
antipattern.reason: By month 6 the actual ICP has often shifted. A stale persona still drives copy. Quarterly review or kill the artifact.
"Who do we say no to?" shapes sales qualification and product scope more concretely than the primary persona. Define it early and link it to the sales-disqualification SOP.

antipattern.description: Asking an LLM to "write a persona for indie SaaS founders" without supplying transcript data.
antipattern.reason: LLMs will produce plausible-sounding frustrations, quotes, and demographics from training data priors rather than your actual customers. Every persona trait must be grounded in a transcript citation.

antipattern.description: Running synthesis at temperature above 0.3 for clustering and trait extraction.
antipattern.reason: Higher temperature fabricates variation that looks like diverse customer segments. Use temperature 0 for clustering; reserve creative phrasing only for the optional characteristic quote field.

antipattern.description: Feeding 30+ interview transcripts into one synthesis call.
antipattern.reason: Long-context dumps cause the model to collapse to averages and lose outliers. Use map-reduce: cluster per transcript, merge cluster summaries, synthesise the merged summaries.

antipattern.description: Including demographic fields (age, city, income) without 5+ transcript agreements.
antipattern.reason: LLMs encode demographic stereotypes from training data. "32-year-old freelance designer in Brooklyn" will appear plausibly even when none of your interviews confirm it. Strip demographics from the synthesis prompt unless the data supports them.

rule.statement: Instruct the synthesis agent: "Include no demographic field unless 5 or more transcripts contain explicit agreement on that dimension."
rule.rationale: Enforces an evidence bar that prevents demographic stereotypes from leaking through model priors.

rule.statement: Run password-scrubber-agent or equivalent before committing any interview transcript to the repository. Reference participants by ID (P07), never name.
rule.rationale: Interview notes contain names, emails, Slack handles, and employer names. PII crossing repo boundaries is a compliance and trust violation, especially for EU-based interviewees under GDPR.

antipattern.description: Always feeding transcripts in the same order to the synthesis agent.
antipattern.reason: LLMs exhibit recency and primacy bias; the first and last transcripts in a batch disproportionately shape the output. Shuffle transcript order between runs; if the persona changes drastically, the data is too thin for a stable finding.

checklist.check: Before committing persona to repo: human reviews all citations; any uncited trait is flagged HALLUCINATION and removed.
Before using persona to drive ad spend: human re-interviews 2 current customers to confirm the persona still holds.
Do not let an agent autonomously update the persona doc on a schedule. Drift detection can be automated; rewrites must not be.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive persona building as a 4-step pipeline. (1) faion-research-agent (mode: personas) ingests interview transcripts from .aidocs/research/interviews/*.md and produces a clustered patterns file. (2) A synthesis pass writes a draft user-personas.md using the lean template. (3) faion-domain-checker-agent is irrelevant here; instead route the draft through faion-marketing-manager for messaging-fit review. (4) Validate by replaying the persona to 3 real customers (human-in-the-loop, do not automate this step).

section.name: limitations-failure-modes
section.p: "Demographic theatre": age/income/photo-stock fields filled in to look complete but never used in any real product/marketing decision. If the persona doesn't change a decision, it's dead weight.
LLM hallucination of quotes: a model asked to "write a persona for indie SaaS founders" will invent plausible-sounding frustrations. Always ground in transcript citations.
Persona drift: shipped once, never updated. By month 6 the actual ICP has shifted but the doc still drives copy. Quarterly review or kill the artifact.
Over-segmentation: 5+ personas across a 3-person team means nobody is the focus. Cap at 1 primary + 1 negative until ARR justifies more.
Confusing personas with segments: a persona is a representative individual; a segment is a group. Don't use persona docs to set ad targeting — use audience-segmentation for that.

list.item: Cite every trait. A persona without [transcript:line] citations is fiction. Treat uncited traits as hypotheses, not facts.
Co-locate persona doc with messaging tests. Each persona's "Headline" field gets an open A/B ticket — if the headline never wins a test, the persona is wrong.
Keep one primary persona until $X ARR threshold (team-defined). Splitting attention between 3 personas at seed-stage is the fastest path to muddled positioning.
Define the negative persona early and link it to your sales-disqualification SOP. "Who do we say no to?" is more decision-shaping than the primary persona.
Use lean template for working personas; only expand to full template when handing off to a marketing or design team that needs context you internalized.
Re-validate every quarter by showing the doc to 3 real customers and asking "does this sound like you?" If two say "no", rebuild.
Pair with jobs-to-be-done. Persona = who, JTBD = why-they-hire-the-product. Both required for a non-trivial product.

template.purpose: Full persona template (demographics, goals, frustrations, day-in-life, tools, buying behavior, messaging)
Lean 6-field persona for working use and quick handoffs
Negative persona template: who NOT to target and why
Python script: regex-based first-draft trait clustering from transcript directory
